records.nearby_text_before: Similarly, the response for logistic regression can be written as a one-hot vector: 0 can map to (1, 0) and 1 can map to (0, 1) One-hot representations are important since they allow us to represent a categorical feature or response as a succinct vector But they have a problem

records.nearby_text_before: Adapter Robustness Ablating one layer's Adaptor makes no difference Adapters on the lower layers have a smaller impact than the higher-layers - those are the crucial ones (read across rows and see last column)
records.nearby_text_after: One intuition is that the lower layers extract lower-level features that are shared among tasks, while the higher layers build features that are unique to different task This indicates that although each adapter has a small influence on the overall network, the overall effect is large The performance of adapters is robust to initialization

records.nearby_text_after: LORA: Low-Rank Adaptation Initialize B to all zeros and A to Gaussian noise so BA = 0 at the start but gradient updates are not trivially 'stuck'
records.text_raw: LORA: For each matrix W in a transformer, for example a key matrix W k , set W k = W k + BA where B , A have low rank r << min { d k , d model } and B ∈ R dk × r , A ∈ R r × dmodel Pretrained Weights WE Raxa
records.text_cleaned: LORA: For each matrix W in a transformer, for example a key matrix $W_k$, set $W_k = W_k + BA$ where $B, A$ have low rank $r \ll \min\{d_k, d_{model}\}$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k \times r}, A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d_{model}}$ Pretrained Weights WE Raxa

B ∈ R dk × r , A ∈ R r × dmodel

records.nearby_text_before: The architecture we used was nice for a few reasons Essentially, we used a BiEncoder for relevance - this is a Two-Tower architecture which produces embeddings such that a search embedding is close to the video embedding This type of architecture is good because we can cache / save embedding and at runtime we just need the query embedding
records.section_title: Anything before we rank? What else?
records.text_raw: A set of videos might be huge and a standard search O ( N ) to find the 10 best, but there are methods to do this much quicker in O (log N ) time
records.text_cleaned: A set of videos might be huge and a standard search O(N) to find the 10 best, but there are methods to do this much quicker in O(log N) time

records.nearby_text_before: Candidate Generator and kNN At this step, we need to find, for a search, the closest videos Here, closest means the videos with the highest inner product to the user's embedding
records.nearby_text_after: Effectively, we want to find the k nearest neighboring videos given a user in terms of their dot product The problem is that finding the nearest neighbors this way, referred to as maximum inner product search (MIPS) across one million videos can take prohibitively long when serving candidates in real time to user Specifically, if there are | V | videos and each vector has dimensions d, finding the top k will be O ( | V | log kd ) (use a max heap)
records.text_raw: Thus, we score videos via a score like q ⊺ v
records.text_cleaned: Thus, we score videos via a score like $q^\top v$

records.nearby_text_before: How does kNN work?
records.nearby_text_after: Approximate kNN algorithms try to reduce the above complexity to O (log | V | ) thereby allowing much faster retrieval High level ideas? Locality sensitive hashing would map each video to a hash... At runtime, the query is projected to a bucket and we only look for videos inside of that bucket
records.text_raw: We have | V | videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be O ( | V | ) which is too expensive
records.text_cleaned: We have |V| videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be O(|V|) which is too expensive

At runtime, the query is projected to a bucket and we only look for videos inside of that bucket Tree based methods also work, and they partition the space of videos into regions... At runtime, the query is projected to a region and similarly we just consider videos in that region

records.nearby_text_before: Re-Ranking? What else? Some ideas on the final ranker
records.nearby_text_after: For example, if we have 100 videos we might break up the 100 into batches of size 10 feed these batches to servers which host the CrossEncoders and then get 100 scores and rank the videos based on these scores Here, we batch since we want to distribute the load but this might not be necessary with the right model + GPU I.e. if the model large, we might need this... If it is small, we might not
records.text_raw: At this step, we can also use a CrossEncoder so that for each candidate video we get a score when we feed in the pair ( q , v ) where q is the query v is the video ... We do this for all videos we need to consider
records.text_cleaned: At this step, we can also use a CrossEncoder so that for each candidate video we get a score when we feed in the pair (q, v) where q is the query and v is the video ... We do this for all videos we need to consider

records.nearby_text_after: We could also have a MLP which takes in the query embedding, the video embedding, a set of normalized video features, and finally produces a score (maybe a probability of click)? This is another idea but notice that we have a lot of features here but many fewer videos There are other cases: we can have multiple rakers where the rankers get more and more 'heavy' (i.e. first we have a Logistic Regression, then an MLP / CrossEncoder hybrid but this last ranker runs on very few videos)

records.nearby_text_before: Issues with DPO? Assumption Mismatch.. DPO is derived from a probabilistic model assuming humans make decisions based on a softmax over reward values. But real human preferences are often noisy and inconsistent. If the assumptions don't hold, the model can misinterpret the preference data
records.nearby_text_after: Sensitivity to Preference Quality... DPO uses pairwise comparisons (e.g., A > B) to guide training. If those preferences are sparse, inconsistent, or biased, the optimization can go astray. There's no reinforcement loop to 'pull back' the model like in PPO (used in RLHF) Less Control Over Output Distribution... Because DPO skips reinforcement-style feedback loops, it may offer less control over certain safety and behavioral constraints that can be more finely tuned with RLHF (e.g., toxicity reduction via explicit reward shaping) Lack of Exploration... In RLHF (especially with PPO), the agent is encouraged to explore and learn from feedback. DPO, being a supervised-style method, does not explicitly explore alternative outputs-it just learns from the comparisons you give it
records.text_raw: Implicit Reward Model ... DPO skips the explicit reward model used in RLHF and optimizes policy directly. This is great for simplicity but means you can't inspect, analyze, or reuse a separate reward model, which can be valuable for understanding model behavior or for multi-stage training setups ... I can't really see the reward even thought I know it is connected to r ∗

records.nearby_text_before: Reusing it across models Improving interpretability Fine-Grained Control via Reward Tuning... Want your model to be funny and safe and informative? RLHF lets you weight these reward components explicitly and dynamically adjust the mix... There is no 'optimal' implicit reward
records.nearby_text_after: Proven Track Record... Many high-profile models (like ChatGPT and Claude) use RLHF because it's a mature and battle-tested method. If you're aiming for production-level robustness, RLHF is still the gold standard

records.nearby_text_before: Why does this work? Let's focus on CBOW and use a window of size 3
records.nearby_text_after: So I have a sentence like 'the queen ruled' and another sentence like 'the king reigned' Given this, we have that the A vectors of ( the, ruled ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of queen We also have that the A vectors of ( the, reigned ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of king
records.text_raw: In this case, we have ( u , v , w ) as 3 words and we use the average A representations of u and w to modify v 's B representation so the inner product we want is large
records.text_cleaned: In this case, we have (u, v, w) as 3 words and we use the average A representations of u and w to modify v's B representation so the inner product we want is large

records.nearby_text_before: Let's focus on CBOW and use a window of size 3 In this case, we have ( u, v, w ) as 3 words and we use the average A representations of u and w to modify v 's B representation so the inner product we want is large So I have a sentence like 'the queen ruled' and another sentence like 'the king reigned'
records.nearby_text_after: We also have that the A vectors of ( the, reigned ) (averaged) is enticed to be like the B vector of king We do this many times, and get that the B vectors of 'queen' and 'king' are both enticed to be similar, since they are similar to the same words Doing this over and over, we have that for any word it's ( a + b ) / 2 vector is similar to all semantically similar words

records.nearby_text_before: Hierarchical Softmax: Another trick to bypass O ( |V| )
records.nearby_text_after: For each leaf unit, there exists a unique path from the root to the unit; and this path is used to estimate the probability of the word represented by the leaf unit Main advantage: we replace the O ( |V| ) Softmax cost with O (log |V| ) The speed of this method depends on how the binary tree is built and words are associated with the leaf nodes; the paper uses a binary Huffman tree
records.section_title: Hierarchical Softmax: Another trick to bypass O ( |V| )

records.nearby_text_before: SGD: Batch size and noisy estimates

records.nearby_text_before: Recurrent Neural Networks: Bidirectional RNN When you are generating text, you can't use a truly bidirectional RNN (given our architecture) since then you are cheating
records.nearby_text_after: Imagine 'He painted the wall with a brush, not a spray can.' x = ( START, he, painted,..., a, spray, can ) y = ( he, painted,..., not, a, spray, can, END )
records.text_raw: For that, you'd need to run a model left-to-right and another (independent) model right-to-left and then fuse the results to get (weak) bidirectionally
records.text_cleaned: For that, you'd need to run a model left-to-right and another (independent) model right-to-left and then fuse the results to get (weak) bidirectionally

records.nearby_text_before: When you are generating text, you can't use a truly bidirectional RNN (given our architecture) since then you are cheating For that, you'd need to run a model left-to-right and another (independent) model right-to-left and then fuse the results to get (weak) bidirectionally Imagine 'He painted the wall with a brush, not a spray can.'
records.nearby_text_after: y = ( he, painted,..., not, a, spray, can, END ) A left-to-right RNN might initially struggle to determine what 'painted the' refers to until it reaches 'wall'. A bidirectional RNN, however, can look ahead and see 'with a brush' while also considering 'painted the', making it easier to correctly infer that 'wall' is the object being painted, rather than, say, 'a picture' or 'a model'.

records.nearby_text_before: At a high level, we want to mix the representations x via Self-Attention so that a position x t is aware of the ones around it This way, we can build a representation that's context-aware If you look at Attention above, you'll notice that it is position invariant! We need to add in positional information
records.nearby_text_after: To disallow this, we add positional embeddings so that we use x t + p t where p t tells us we are at the start, middle, etc of the sentence To help with gradients, we add residual connections and layer norm (LN) After each Self-Attention application, we pass each embedding through a Feed-Forward Neural Network with a ReLU activation that projects each token's embedding up to 4 ∗ d model dimension and then back down to d model space

records.nearby_text_before: Knowledge Distillation What's the idea of Knowledge Distillation?
records.nearby_text_after: You want the student answers to be similar to the teacher answers Case 1: If the teacher is too sure ( p (1) j is high for one particular j ), T →∞ smooths outs the distribution so p ( T ) becomes uniform; this adds a bit of hedging Case 2: If you want to just learn from the teacher directly, you can use T ∼ 0 and in the limit you use y t j = 1 if j = argmax ( p i ) otherwise y t j = 0
records.section_title: What's the idea of Knowledge Distillation?
records.text_raw: You want the student to still learn things from the teacher; here, 'learns' means same probabilities per target
